=== PLAN ===
Build a browser-based UI for Tiny Macro Lisp on COR24. Phase 1: compile tml24c to WASM via Emscripten or rewrite core in Rust. Phase 2: REPL component. Phase 3: visual execution trace.

=== STEPS ===
  [ ] 001-research-and-scaffold [production]: Study tml24c at ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c (read CLAUDE.md, docs/, src/).... <-- YOU ARE HERE
  [ ] 002-lisp-wasm [legacy]: Get Lisp evaluator running in WASM
  [ ] 003-repl [legacy]: Build interactive REPL component
  [ ] 004-examples [legacy]: Add example Lisp programs with one-click load
  [ ] 005-trace [legacy]: Visual execution trace showing eval steps

=== CURRENT STEP: 001-research-and-scaffold [production] ===
Status: pending
Description: Study tml24c at ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c (read CLAUDE.md, docs/, src/)....

=== PROMPT ===
Study tml24c at ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tml24c (read CLAUDE.md, docs/, src/). Study cor24-rs at ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs and tc24r at ~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r. Then: (1) Decide approach: compile tml24c C code to WASM via Emscripten, or create a Rust wrapper around the Lisp core. Document decision in docs/architecture.md. (2) Create the project structure (Rust/Yew with trunk, or C with Emscripten). (3) Create a minimal REPL component: text input, eval button, output area. (4) Verify it builds.

=== WHEN DONE ===
Run: agentrail begin    (if not yet started)
Run: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --next-slug <slug> --next-prompt "next instructions"
  Or: agentrail complete --summary "what you did" --done   (if this is the last step)

1→# tml24c
     2→
     3→Tiny Macro Lisp compiled to the COR24 toolchain.
     4→
     5→A minimal Lisp system that compiles to COR24 assembly, using:
     6→- [tc24r](https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r) -- Tiny COR24 compiler in Rust
     7→- [cor24-rs](https://github.com/sw-embed/cor24-rs) -- COR24 assembler and emulator
     8→
     9→## Status
    10→
    11→Project scaffolding. Development managed by [AgentRail](https://github.com/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-rs).
    12→
    13→## License
    14→
    15→MIT
    16→

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## CRITICAL: AgentRail Session Protocol (MUST follow exactly)
     6→
     7→This project uses AgentRail. Every session follows this exact sequence:
     8→
     9→### 1. START (do this FIRST, before anything else)
    10→```bash
    11→agentrail next
    12→```
    13→Read the output carefully. It tells you your current step, prompt, skill docs, and past trajectories.
    14→
    15→### 2. BEGIN (immediately after reading the next output)
    16→```bash
    17→agentrail begin
    18→```
    19→
    20→### 3. WORK (do what the step prompt says)
    21→Do NOT ask the user "want me to proceed?" or "shall I start?". The step prompt IS your instruction. Execute it.
    22→
    23→### 4. COMMIT (after the work is done)
    24→Commit your code changes with git.
    25→
    26→### 5. COMPLETE (LAST thing, after committing)
    27→```bash
    28→agentrail complete --summary "what you accomplished" \
    29→  --reward 1 \
    30→  --actions "tools and approach used"
    31→```
    32→If the step failed: `--reward -1 --failure-mode "what went wrong"`
    33→If the saga is finished: add `--done`
    34→
    35→Do NOT skip steps 1, 2, or 5. The next session depends on your trajectory recording.
    36→
    37→## Project
    38→
    39→Tiny Macro Lisp (tml24c): a minimal Lisp-1 with lexical scope, unhygienic defmacro, closures, and mark-sweep GC. Compiles to COR24 24-bit RISC assembly.
    40→
    41→## Related Projects
    42→
    43→- `~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs` -- COR24 assembler and emulator (Rust)
    44→- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/tc24r` -- Tiny COR24 compiler (Rust)
    45→- `~/github/sw-vibe-coding/agentrail-domain-coding` -- Coding skills domain
    46→
    47→## Available Task Types
    48→
    49→`c-project-init`, `c-compile-fix`, `lisp-define-form`, `pre-commit`
    50→
    51→## Build
    52→
    53→```bash
    54→make          # build
    55→make clean    # clean
    56→```
    57→
    58→Compiler flags: `-Wall -Wextra -Werror -std=c11`. Never suppress warnings.
    59→

1→# tc24r - C compiler for COR24 (24-bit RISC) ISA
     2→
     3→## Build & Test
     4→
     5→- No workspace-level Cargo.toml; build individual components:
     6→  `cargo build --manifest-path components/cli/Cargo.toml --release`
     7→- Run reg-rs regression tests: `reg-rs run -p tc24r`
     8→- Run a single demo: `bash demos/run-demo.sh`
     9→
    10→## Key Rules
    11→
    12→- **Never use `sed` in test scripts or demo scripts.** Use `awk`, `grep`, and `tr` instead. BSD sed (macOS) and GNU sed (Linux) have incompatible extensions (`\s`, `-E` vs `-r`, etc.) that break cross-platform testing. All demo scripts have been ported to use `awk` for field extraction.
    13→- reg-rs baselines (`.out` files) contain absolute paths. After switching machines, run `reg-rs rebase -p tc24r` to update baselines.
    14→
    15→## Project Structure
    16→
    17→- `components/` - Rust crates organized by compiler phase (core, frontend, backend, codegen-*, cli, testing)
    18→- `demos/` - 46 end-to-end demo scripts (run-demo*.sh) with C source files
    19→- `work/reg-rs/` - reg-rs regression test definitions (.rgt) and baselines (.out)
    20→- `docs/` - Design docs, testing strategy, known issues
    21→

1→# Architecture: Tiny Macro Lisp (tml24c) 2→ 3→## System Overview 4→ 5→``` 6→ ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────┐ 7→ │ tml24c Runtime │ 8→ │ │ 9→ UART input ──────► Reader ──► S-expression (Value tree) │ 10→ │ │ │ 11→ │ ▼ │ 12→ │ Macro Expand │ 13→ │ │ │ 14→ │ ▼ │ 15→ │ Evaluator ◄──── Environment │ 16→ │ │ (lexical) │ 17→ │ ▼ │ 18→ │ Printer ────────► UART output │ 19→ │ │ 20→ │ ┌─── Heap ───┐ │ 21→ │ │ cons pairs │ │ 22→ │ │ symbols │◄──── GC (mark-sweep) │ 23→ │ │ strings │ │ 24→ │ │ closures │ │ 25→ │ │ macros │ │ 26→ │ └─────────────┘ │ 27→ │ │ 28→ │ Platform Abstraction Layer │ 29→ └──────────┬───────────────────────┬───────────┘ 30→ │ │ 31→ ┌──────────▼──────┐ ┌─────────▼──────────┐ 32→ │ Host (gcc) │ │ COR24 (tc24r) │ 33→ │ stdio putchar │ │ UART MMIO │ 34→ │ malloc arena │ │ SRAM heap │ 35→ └─────────────────┘ └────────────────────┘ 36→``` 37→ 38→## Components 39→ 40→### Reader (`read.c`) 41→Converts text input into Value trees (S-expressions). 42→ 43→**Tokens:** `(`, `)`, `'`, `` ` ``, `,`, `,@`, `"string"`, integers, symbols. 44→ 45→**Produces:** Tagged Value objects — fixnums (immediate), symbols and lists (heap-allocated cons pairs). 46→ 47→### Evaluator (`eval.c`) 48→Tree-walking interpreter. Dispatches on value type and special form tag. 49→ 50→**Special forms:** 51→- `quote` — return unevaluated 52→- `if` — conditional 53→- `define` — bind in current environment 54→- `set!` — mutate existing binding 55→- `lambda` — create closure (captures environment) 56→- `defmacro` — create macro object 57→- `do` / `progn` — sequential evaluation 58→- `let` / `let*` — local bindings (may be macros over lambda) 59→- `quasiquote` — template with unquote/splice 60→ 61→**Function application:** Evaluate operator, evaluate arguments, extend closure's captured environment with bindings, evaluate body. 62→ 63→### Macro Expander (in `eval.c`) 64→Before normal evaluation, check if the operator is a macro. If so: 65→1. Apply macro function to **unevaluated** arguments 66→2. Get expanded form 67→3. Evaluate the expansion 68→ 69→Single-pass expand-then-eval (no separate expansion phase initially). 70→ 71→### Environment (`env.c`) 72→Lexical scope via chained environment frames. 73→ 74→``` 75→┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐ 76→│ frame 3 │────►│ frame 2 │────►│ global │ 77→│ x = 10 │ │ y = 20 │ │ + = │ 78→│ z = 30 │ │ │ │ cons =

│ 79→└─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ └─────────────┘ 80→``` 81→ 82→Each frame is a list of (symbol . value) pairs linked to a parent frame. Lookup walks the chain outward. 83→ 84→### Garbage Collector (`gc.c`) 85→Mark-sweep over a single contiguous heap. 86→ 87→**Roots:** 88→- Global environment 89→- Current evaluation stack (temp roots) 90→- Symbol table 91→- Reader/printer temporaries 92→ 93→**Mark phase:** Recursive traversal from roots, setting mark bit on each reachable object. 94→ 95→**Sweep phase:** Linear scan of heap, free unmarked objects to free list. 96→ 97→**Trigger:** When allocation fails (heap exhausted), run GC. If still insufficient, report out-of-memory. 98→ 99→### Printer (`print.c`) 100→Converts Value trees back to text. Handles: fixnums, symbols, lists (proper and dotted), strings, `#`, `#`, `#`. 101→ 102→### Symbol Table (`symbol.c`) 103→Interned symbols — each unique name has exactly one symbol object. Comparison is pointer equality. 104→ 105→### Heap (`heap.c`) 106→Fixed-size arena of cells. Initially bump allocation (no GC). Later: free-list with mark-sweep. 107→ 108→### Platform Abstraction (`platform.h`) 109→```c 110→void platform_putchar(int ch); 111→int platform_getchar(void); 112→void platform_halt(int code); 113→``` 114→ 115→**Host:** Maps to `putchar`/`getchar`/`exit`. 116→**COR24:** Maps to UART MMIO at 0xFF0100 with TX-busy polling. 117→ 118→## Memory Model 119→ 120→### Host Build 121→Standard C memory. Heap is a static array (`Value heap[HEAP_SIZE]`). Stack is C call stack. 122→ 123→### COR24 Build 124→ 125→``` 126→0x000000 ┌─────────────────┐ 127→ │ Code (.text) │ Program + string literals 128→ ├─────────────────┤ 129→ │ Data (.data) │ Initialized globals 130→ ├─────────────────┤ 131→ │ BSS (.comm) │ Symbol table, heap array 132→ │ │ 133→ │ ▼ Heap grows up │ 134→ │ │ 135→ │ │ 136→0x0FFFFF └─────────────────┘ End of SRAM (1 MB) 137→ 138→0xFEE000 ┌─────────────────┐ 139→ │ │ 140→ │ ▲ Stack grows dn │ 141→ │ │ 142→0xFEEC00 └─────────────────┘ Initial SP (top of EBR) 143→ 144→0xFF0100 UART_DATA (TX/RX) 145→0xFF0101 UART_STAT 146→``` 147→ 148→**Heap sizing:** With 1 MB SRAM, after code (~10–20 KB estimated), the heap can be 200–900 KB. Each cons cell = 6 bytes (two 24-bit tagged values). A 64K-cell heap = 384 KB. 149→ 150→### Tagged Value Representation 151→ 152→Every Lisp value is a single 24-bit word with tag bits in the low 2 bits: 153→ 154→| Tag (bits 1:0) | Type | Payload (bits 23:2) | 155→|-----------------|------|---------------------| 156→| 00 | Fixnum | 22-bit signed integer | 157→| 01 | Cons pointer | Heap address >> 1 (word-aligned) | 158→| 10 | Symbol pointer | Heap address >> 1 | 159→| 11 | Other pointer | Closure, string, primitive, macro | 160→ 161→**Fixnum range:** -2,097,152 to 2,097,151 (22-bit signed). 162→ 163→**Special values:** 164→- `NIL` = symbol pointer to the interned `nil` symbol (or a dedicated sentinel) 165→- `T` = symbol pointer to the interned `t` symbol 166→ 167→### Heap Object Layout 168→ 169→**Cons cell (6 bytes):** 170→``` 171→┌──────────┬──────────┐ 172→│ car (3B) │ cdr (3B) │ Both are tagged Values 173→└──────────┴──────────┘ 174→``` 175→ 176→**Symbol (6+ bytes):** 177→``` 178→┌──────────┬──────────┐ 179→│ name ptr │ next sym │ name = pointer to string, next = symbol chain 180→└──────────┴──────────┘ 181→``` 182→ 183→**Closure (9 bytes):** 184→``` 185→┌──────────┬──────────┬──────────┐ 186→│ params │ body │ env │ All tagged Values (lists/pointers) 187→└──────────┴──────────┴──────────┘ 188→``` 189→ 190→**String (3 + n bytes):** 191→``` 192→┌──────────┬──────────────────┐ 193→│ length │ chars[0..n-1] │ length = fixnum, chars = byte array 194→└──────────┴──────────────────┘ 195→``` 196→ 197→**GC header:** Each heap object needs a mark bit + type tag for the collector. Options: 198→- Steal 1 bit from the object header 199→- Use a separate bitmap (1 bit per cell) 200→- Use a type byte prefix before each object 201→ 202→Design decision deferred to implementation — the separate bitmap approach avoids disturbing object layout. 203→ 204→## Data Flow 205→ 206→### REPL Loop 207→``` 208→loop: 209→ print("> ") 210→ expr = read(stdin) 211→ if expr == EOF: halt 212→ result = eval(expr, global_env) 213→ print(result) 214→ println() 215→ goto loop 216→``` 217→ 218→### Macro Expansion (in eval) 219→``` 220→eval(expr, env): 221→ if expr is symbol: lookup(expr, env) 222→ if expr is atom: return expr 223→ // expr is a list: (op args...) 224→ op_val = eval(car(expr), env) 225→ if op_val is macro: 226→ expanded = apply(op_val, cdr(expr)) // unevaluated args 227→ return eval(expanded, env) // eval the expansion 228→ else: 229→ args = eval_list(cdr(expr), env) 230→ return apply(op_val, args) 231→``` 232→ 233→## Build System 234→ 235→### Host Build 236→```makefile 237→CC = cc 238→CFLAGS = -Wall -Wextra -Werror -std=c11 239→SRCS = main.c eval.c read.c print.c heap.c symbol.c env.c builtin.c gc.c platform_host.c 240→``` 241→ 242→### COR24 Build (future) 243→```makefile 244→CC = tc24r 245→# Single-file compilation: concatenate or #include all sources 246→# Output: tml24c.s → as24 → tml24c.lgo 247→``` 248→ 249→tc24r requires single-file input, so the COR24 build will either: 250→1. Use `#include` to pull all `.c` files into one translation unit, or 251→2. Concatenate sources with a build script 252→ 253→## Dependencies 254→ 255→- **cor24-rs** (assembler + emulator) — for COR24 target builds and testing 256→- **tc24r** (C compiler) — for COR24 target compilation 257→- **gcc/clang** — for host development and testing 258→- **make** — build system 259→

1→# Product Requirements: Tiny Macro Lisp (tml24c)
     2→
     3→## Purpose
     4→
     5→A minimal Lisp-1 with lexical scope, unhygienic `defmacro`, closures, and mark-sweep GC. Written in C, targeting COR24 24-bit RISC architecture. Serves three goals:
     6→
     7→1. **Learn** — First garbage-collected interpreter implementation
     8→2. **Validate** — Stress-test tc24r C compiler, cor24-rs assembler/emulator on non-trivial software
     9→3. **Use** — Provide a programmable language for COR24 embedded apps
    10→
    11→## Target Demos
    12→
    13→### Must demonstrate
    14→- **Anaphoric macros:** `aif`, `awhen` — the flagship macro system demo
    15→- **List processing:** `map`, `filter`, `foldl` over cons lists
    16→- **Recursive functions:** Fibonacci, factorial, list-length, append
    17→- **Closures:** `make-adder`, counter with captured state
    18→- **Arithmetic:** Integer math on 22-bit fixnums (tagged 24-bit words)
    19→- **REPL:** Interactive read-eval-print loop over UART
    20→- **File/string eval:** Load and evaluate Lisp source from embedded strings
    21→
    22→### Should demonstrate
    23→- **String operations:** Literals, concatenation, comparison, print
    24→- **Bootstrap library:** `when`, `unless`, `cond`, `and`, `or` defined in Lisp
    25→- **Garbage collection:** Programs that allocate heavily and survive GC cycles
    26→- **Quasiquote:** Backquote/unquote/unquote-splicing for macro definitions
    27→
    28→### Nice to have
    29→- **Board demo:** LED control or UART echo app written in Lisp
    30→- **Threading macros:** `->`, `->>` (Clojure-inspired)
    31→- **`macroexpand-1`:** Inspect macro expansion interactively
    32→
    33→## Constraints
    34→
    35→### Hardware
    36→- **ISA:** COR24 — 24-bit RISC, 8 registers (3 GP), variable-length instructions
    37→- **Memory:** 1 MB SRAM + 3 KB EBR; heap must fit in available SRAM after code
    38→- **I/O:** UART only (0xFF0100), no filesystem
    39→- **No hardware divide** — division via runtime helpers (repeated subtraction)
    40→- **No FPU** — integer-only arithmetic
    41→
    42→### Toolchain
    43→- **C compiler:** tc24r — C99 subset, single-file, no float, no varargs, no bitfields
    44→- **Assembler:** cor24-rs `as24` — two-pass, LGO output
    45→- **Emulator:** cor24-rs — step/run/breakpoint, UART capture
    46→- **Host testing:** Must also compile with gcc/clang on host for development
    47→
    48→### Language constraints (from research)
    49→- No hygienic macros, no package system, no CLOS, no continuations
    50→- No bignums, ratios, or complex numeric tower
    51→- No bytecode VM initially — tree-walking interpreter
    52→- No tail-call optimization initially (add if time permits)
    53→
    54→## Success Criteria
    55→
    56→### Phase 1: Host Bootstrap (MVP)
    57→- [ ] Evaluator handles: quote, if, define, lambda, function application
    58→- [ ] Reader parses: integers, symbols, lists, quote shorthand
    59→- [ ] Printer outputs: integers, symbols, lists
    60→- [ ] Builtins: cons, car, cdr, eq?, atom?, +, -, *, <, print
    61→- [ ] Lexical closures work (make-adder test passes)
    62→- [ ] Compiles and runs on host (gcc/clang)
    63→
    64→### Phase 2: Full Language
    65→- [ ] Mark-sweep GC handles allocation pressure
    66→- [ ] `defmacro` + quasiquote + gensym work
    67→- [ ] `when`, `unless`, `cond`, `and`, `or` defined in Lisp
    68→- [ ] `aif`, `awhen` anaphoric macros work
    69→- [ ] `map`, `filter`, `foldl` work
    70→- [ ] String type with basic operations
    71→
    72→### Phase 3: COR24 Target
    73→- [ ] Compiles with tc24r (single-file build)
    74→- [ ] Runs on cor24-rs emulator
    75→- [ ] UART REPL works
    76→- [ ] GC stress test passes on emulator
    77→- [ ] Macro demo runs end-to-end
    78→
    79→### Phase 4: Integration & Polish
    80→- [ ] Board demo (FPGA or emulator)
    81→- [ ] Bootstrap library loaded from embedded strings
    82→- [ ] Documentation complete
    83→

1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Build Commands
     6→
     7→**CRITICAL: NEVER run `trunk` commands directly.** Always use the shell scripts below. Running bare `trunk serve` or `trunk build` with wrong flags breaks the build (wrong port, missing `--release`, wrong `--public-url`). The scripts encode the correct arguments.
     8→
     9→```bash
    10→# Dev server with hot reload (http://localhost:7401/cor24-rs/)
    11→./serve.sh              # incremental build + serve
    12→./serve.sh --clean      # clean build + serve (use after strange build errors)
    13→
    14→# Production build (outputs to pages/)
    15→./build.sh              # incremental build
    16→./build.sh --clean      # clean build
    17→
    18→# Run tests (OK to run cargo directly for non-build commands)
    19→cargo test
    20→
    21→# Check compilation (OK to run cargo directly)
    22→cargo check
    23→cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown   # checks WASM-only code too
    24→cargo clippy --target wasm32-unknown-unknown  # lint check
    25→```
    26→
    27→Prerequisites: Rust 1.75+, Trunk (`cargo install trunk`), `rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown`.
    28→
    29→## Commit Discipline
    30→
    31→**Commit early and often.** Each commit should do one thing. Do not accumulate large changesets.
    32→
    33→- Commit after each logical change: a bug fix, a new feature, a refactor, an extraction — each is its own commit.
    34→- Small commits enable cherry-picking, rebasing, and bisecting. Large commits make all of these painful.
    35→- If a task involves multiple steps (e.g., extract data to files, then update callers, then add a new feature), commit after each step.
    36→- Commit working code. Run `cargo check --target wasm32-unknown-unknown` before committing WASM changes.
    37→- Deployment commits (`pages/` updates via `./build.sh`) should be separate from code changes when practical.
    38→
    39→## Deployment
    40→
    41→The `pages/` directory contains pre-built production assets and is committed to git. GitHub Actions deploys from `pages/` on push to `main` — no CI build step, just upload.
    42→
    43→**To deploy changes to the live site:**
    44→1. `./build.sh --clean` — always use `--clean` to avoid stale cached WASM artifacts
    45→2. `git add pages/` and commit (separate from code changes)
    46→3. `git push`
    47→
    48→**IMPORTANT:** Use `./build.sh --clean`, not `./build.sh`. Incremental builds can serve stale code if `include_str!()` data files changed but Rust source didn't.
    49→
    50→## Architecture
    51→
    52→This is a browser-based COR24 CPU emulator written in Rust, compiled to WebAssembly via Trunk. The COR24 is a real 24-bit RISC architecture (C-Oriented RISC) designed for embedded systems education.
    53→
    54→### Workspace Structure
    55→
    56→- **`src/`** — Main application crate (`cor24-emulator`)
    57→- **`components/`** — Reusable Yew UI components library
    58→- **`rust-to-cor24/`** — Standalone CLI tool (not part of workspace). Pipeline: Rust → `rustc --target msp430-none-elf --emit asm` → MSP430 ASM → `msp430-to-cor24 --entry <func>` → COR24 ASM (with `bra <entry>` reset vector prologue at address 0). See `rust-to-cor24/README.md` for full pipeline documentation. Not compiled to WASM — used offline to generate pipeline examples shown in the Web UI's Rust tab.
    59→
    60→### Core Modules (src/)
    61→
    62→- **`cpu/`** — CPU emulator core
    63→  - `state.rs` — CPU state, memory (64KB subset of 24-bit address space), memory-mapped I/O (LED/switch at `0xFF0000`, UART at `0xFFFF00-02`)
    64→  - `executor.rs` — Instruction execution engine
    65→  - `decode_rom.rs` — Decode ROM extracted from actual FPGA Verilog hardware
    66→  - `encode.rs` — Instruction encoding tables
    67→  - `instruction.rs` — Opcode definitions, variable-length instructions (1/2/4 bytes)
    68→- **`assembler.rs`** — Two-pass assembler producing machine code from COR24 assembly
    69→- **`wasm.rs`** — `WasmCpu` wrapper exposing CPU to JavaScript/Yew via `wasm_bindgen`
    70→- **`app.rs`** — Main Yew `#[function_component(App)]` — all application state and UI logic. This is the largest file; it manages two independent CPU instances (assembler tab and Rust pipeline tab)
    71→- **`challenge.rs`** — Example programs and challenge definitions
    72→
    73→### UI Components (components/)
    74→
    75→Yew components: `Header`, `Sidebar`, `TabBar`, `ProgramArea`, `RegisterPanel`, `MemoryViewer`, `Modal`, `Collapsible`, `RustPipeline`. The `RustPipeline` component implements a wizard-driven 3-column view showing the Rust→MSP430 ASM→COR24 ASM→Machine Code pipeline with pre-built examples.
    76→
    77→### Key Patterns
    78→
    79→- **Two CPU instances**: `app.rs` maintains separate `WasmCpu` state for the Assembler tab and Rust Pipeline tab
    80→- **Animated run with stop**: Uses `Rc<Cell<bool>>` for stop flags and `Rc<Cell<u8>>` for switch state to ensure immediate visibility across async closures (Yew state updates are deferred)
    81→- **Hardware-accurate I/O**: Matches COR24-TB test board — single LED (D2) and button (S2) using bit 0 of `IO_LEDSWDAT` (`0xFF0000`). Reference hardware docs are in `references/COR24-TB/`
    82→- **Conditional compilation**: `app.rs` and `wasm.rs` are `#[cfg(target_arch = "wasm32")]` only; `cpu/`, `assembler`, and `challenge` modules compile on native targets for `cargo test`
    83→- **`build.rs`**: Embeds git SHA, build timestamp, and hostname into the binary via env vars
    84→
    85→### CSS
    86→
    87→Two stylesheet files in `styles/`: `asm-game.css` (component styles) and `layout.css` (page structure). Referenced in `index.html` via Trunk's `data-trunk` attributes.
    88→
    89→### Reference Materials
    90→
    91→`references/COR24-TB/` contains the actual hardware documentation: Verilog source (including `cor24_io.v` for I/O address decoding), demo C programs (blinky, sieve, etc.), and FPGA project files. The decode ROM in `decode_rom.rs` was extracted from `cor24_cpu.v` using `scripts/extract_decode_rom.py`.
    92→

1→# MakerLisp COR24 — Assembly Emulator
     2→
     3→A browser-based educational emulator for the
     4→[MakerLisp](https://makerlisp.com) COR24 (C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit)
     5→architecture. Written in Rust and compiled to WebAssembly.
     6→
     7→**[Live Demo](https://sw-embed.github.io/cor24-rs/)**
     8→
     9→### Assembler Tab
    10→![COR24 Assembler Tab](images/assembler-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)
    11→
    12→### C Tab
    13→![COR24 C Pipeline Tab](images/c-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)
    14→
    15→### Rust Tab
    16→![COR24 Rust Pipeline Tab](images/rust-tab.png?ts=1774129001000)
    17→
    18→### CLI Pipeline Demos
    19→
    20→![Assembler UART Hello](docs/vhs/asm-uart-hello.gif?ts=1774129001000)
    21→
    22→![Rust Pipeline UART Hello](docs/vhs/uart-hello.gif?ts=1774129001000)
    23→
    24→## Features
    25→
    26→- **Interactive Assembly Editor** — Write and edit COR24 assembly code
    27→- **Step-by-Step Execution** — Debug your code instruction by instruction
    28→- **Multi-Region Memory Viewer** — Program, Stack, and I/O regions with change heatmaps
    29→- **CLI Debugger** (`cor24-dbg`) — GDB-like command-line debugger with breakpoints, UART, and LED/button I/O
    30→- **LGO File Loader** — Load programs assembled with the reference `as24` toolchain
    31→- **Built-in Examples** — Learn from pre-loaded example programs
    32→- **Challenges** — Test your assembly skills with programming challenges
    33→- **ISA Reference** — Instruction set documentation and memory map
    34→
    35→## COR24 Architecture
    36→
    37→COR24 is a 24-bit RISC soft CPU for Lattice MachXO FPGAs, designed for
    38→embedded systems education. 32 operations encode into 211 instruction
    39→forms (1, 2, or 4 bytes).
    40→
    41→- **3 General-Purpose Registers**: r0, r1, r2 (24-bit)
    42→- **5 Special-Purpose Registers**:
    43→  - r3 = fp (frame pointer)
    44→  - r4 = sp (stack pointer, init 0xFEEC00)
    45→  - r5 = z (always zero; usable only in compare instructions)
    46→  - r6 = iv (interrupt vector)
    47→  - r7 = ir (interrupt return address)
    48→- **Single Condition Flag**: C (set by compare instructions)
    49→- **16 MB Address Space**: 1 MB SRAM + 3 KB EBR (stack) + memory-mapped I/O
    50→- **Variable-Length Instructions**: 1, 2, or 4 bytes
    51→
    52→### Supported Instructions
    53→
    54→| Category | Instructions |
    55→|----------|-------------|
    56→| Arithmetic | `add`, `sub`, `mul` |
    57→| Logic | `and`, `or`, `xor` |
    58→| Shifts | `shl`, `sra`, `srl` |
    59→| Compare | `ceq`, `cls`, `clu` |
    60→| Branch | `bra`, `brf`, `brt` |
    61→| Jump | `jmp`, `jal` |
    62→| Load | `la`, `lc`, `lcu`, `lb`, `lbu`, `lw` |
    63→| Store | `sb`, `sw` |
    64→| Stack | `push`, `pop` |
    65→| Move | `mov`, `sxt`, `zxt` |
    66→
    67→## Examples & Demos
    68→
    69→This project has two sets of examples, matching the two tabs in the web UI:
    70→
    71→- **[Assembler Examples](docs/assembler-examples.md)** — 11 hand-written COR24 assembly programs.
    72→  Available in the web UI's **Assembler** tab (click Examples → pick one → Assemble → Run)
    73→  and via `cor24-dbg` on the command line (see `scripts/demo-cli-*.sh`).
    74→
    75→- **[Rust Pipeline Demos](docs/rust-pipeline-demos.md)** — 12 Rust programs compiled through the
    76→  Rust → MSP430 → COR24 cross-compilation pipeline.
    77→  Available in the web UI's **Rust** tab (pick example → Compile → Translate → Assemble → Run)
    78→  and via CLI scripts in `rust-to-cor24/demos/` (see `run-demo.sh`, per-demo `run.sh`, `generate-all.sh`).
    79→
    80→```bash
    81→# Assembler example via CLI debugger
    82→scripts/demo-cli-hello-world.sh
    83→
    84→# Rust pipeline demo via CLI
    85→rust-to-cor24/demos/run-demo.sh demo_add
    86→rust-to-cor24/demos/run-demo.sh demo_echo_v2 --uart-input 'hello!'
    87→```
    88→
    89→For an overview of all the binaries and how they fit together, see **[docs/eli5.md](docs/eli5.md)**.
    90→
    91→## Building
    92→
    93→### Prerequisites
    94→
    95→- [Rust](https://rustup.rs/) (1.75+)
    96→- [Trunk](https://trunkrs.dev/) (`cargo install trunk`)
    97→- wasm32-unknown-unknown target (`rustup target add wasm32-unknown-unknown`)
    98→
    99→### Development
   100→
   101→```bash
   102→# Serve locally with hot reload (port 7401)
   103→./serve.sh
   104→
   105→# Or directly:
   106→trunk serve --port 7401
   107→
   108→# Open http://localhost:7401/cor24-rs/
   109→```
   110→
   111→### Production Build
   112→
   113→```bash
   114→# Build optimized WASM to pages/
   115→trunk build --release
   116→```
   117→
   118→## Project Structure
   119→
   120→```
   121→cor24-rs/
   122→├── src/
   123→│   ├── cpu/           # CPU emulator core
   124→│   │   ├── decode_rom.rs  # Instruction decode ROM (from hardware)
   125→│   │   ├── encode.rs      # Instruction encoding tables

1→# tc24r Architecture
     2→
     3→## Component Layout
     4→
     5→The compiler is split into 4 components, each a Cargo workspace with
     6→multiple crates under a `crates/` directory:
     7→
     8→```
     9→components/
    10→  core/                          -- shared types
    11→    crates/
    12→      tc24r-ast/                  -- AST node types (Program, Function, Stmt, Expr, Type)
    13→      tc24r-error/                -- CompileError type
    14→      tc24r-span/                 -- Source location (file, line, column)
    15→      tc24r-token/                -- TokenKind enum and Token struct
    16→
    17→  frontend/                      -- source text to AST
    18→    crates/
    19→      tc24r-preprocess/           -- #define, #include, #pragma once
    20→      tc24r-preprocess-tests/     -- preprocessor test suite
    21→      tc24r-lexer/                -- tokenizer (operators, readers, main loop)
    22→      tc24r-lexer-tests/          -- lexer test suite
    23→      tc24r-parse-stream/         -- token stream with expect/peek helpers
    24→      tc24r-parser/               -- recursive-descent parser
    25→      tc24r-parser-tests/         -- parser test suite
    26→
    27→  backend/                       -- AST to assembly
    28→    crates/
    29→      tc24r-codegen/              -- COR24 assembly emitter
    30→      tc24r-codegen-tests/        -- golden file tests and codegen checks
    31→      tc24r-codegen-validate/     -- assembler validation (cor24-run and as24)
    32→
    33→  cli/                           -- binary entry point
    34→    crates/
    35→      tc24r/                      -- main.rs, argument parsing, orchestration
    36→```
    37→
    38→14 crates total. Test crates are separated from library crates so that
    39→test dependencies do not affect the library build.
    40→
    41→## Data Flow
    42→
    43→Source code flows through the compiler in a linear pipeline:
    44→
    45→```
    46→input.c
    47→  |
    48→  v
    49→[preprocess] -- #define substitution, #include expansion, #pragma once
    50→  |
    51→  v
    52→preprocessed source text
    53→  |
    54→  v
    55→[lexer] -- tokenize into TokenKind stream
    56→  |
    57→  v
    58→token stream
    59→  |
    60→  v
    61→[parser] -- recursive descent, produces AST
    62→  |
    63→  v
    64→AST (Program -> Vec<Function> + Vec<Global>)
    65→  |
    66→  v
    67→[codegen] -- walk AST, emit COR24 assembly text
    68→  |
    69→  v
    70→output.s
    71→```
    72→
    73→There is no intermediate representation (IR). The AST is walked directly
    74→by the code generator to emit assembly, following the chibicc approach.
    75→
    76→## Crate Dependencies
    77→
    78→Dependencies flow in one direction: cli -> backend -> frontend -> core.
    79→
    80→```
    81→tc24r (cli)
    82→  |
    83→  +-- tc24r-codegen
    84→  |     +-- tc24r-ast
    85→  |     +-- tc24r-error
    86→  |     +-- tc24r-span
    87→  |
    88→  +-- tc24r-parser
    89→  |     +-- tc24r-ast
    90→  |     +-- tc24r-token
    91→  |     +-- tc24r-parse-stream
    92→  |           +-- tc24r-token
    93→  |           +-- tc24r-span
    94→  |           +-- tc24r-error
    95→  |
    96→  +-- tc24r-lexer
    97→  |     +-- tc24r-token
    98→  |     +-- tc24r-span
    99→  |
   100→  +-- tc24r-preprocess
   101→        +-- tc24r-span
   102→```
   103→
   104→## Design Constraints
   105→
   106→The codebase follows size constraints to keep modules focused:
   107→
   108→- **< 5 public functions per module** -- each .rs file has a narrow API
   109→- **< 5 modules per crate** -- crates stay small and single-purpose
   110→- **Prefer many small crates** over few large ones
   111→- **Test crates are separate** -- tc24r-lexer-tests, tc24r-parser-tests, etc.
   112→- **No circular dependencies** -- strictly layered
   113→
   114→## Key Design Decisions
   115→
   116→### No IR
   117→
   118→The compiler emits assembly directly from the AST. This keeps the codebase
   119→small and follows chibicc's architecture. The tradeoff is that optimization
   120→passes would need to work on either the AST or the assembly text.
   121→
   122→### Push/Pop Expression Evaluation
   123→
   124→Binary expressions are evaluated by:
   125→1. Evaluate left-hand side into r0
   126→2. Push r0
   127→3. Evaluate right-hand side into r0
   128→4. Pop r1 (left-hand side)
   129→5. Apply operator: r0 = r1 op r0
   130→
   131→This is simple and correct but generates more push/pop pairs than necessary.
   132→A future peephole pass could eliminate redundant stack traffic.
   133→
   134→### Stack-Allocated Locals
   135→
   136→At function entry, a pre-pass counts all local variable declarations
   137→(including arrays) and allocates stack space with a single `sub sp,N`.
   138→Variables are accessed at negative offsets from fp. Array elements are
   139→contiguous starting from the variable's base offset.
   140→
   141→### Label Generation
   142→
   143→Labels are generated from a monotonic counter: L0, L1, L2, etc. Each
   144→control-flow construct consumes one or more labels. The counter is global
   145→across all functions in a translation unit.
   146→
   147→### Software Division
   148→
   149→COR24 has no hardware divide instruction. When the compiler encounters
   150→`/` or `%`, it emits calls to `__tc24r_div` or `__tc24r_mod` helper
   151→functions. These are emitted once at the end of the assembly output
   152→if used.
   153→
   154→## Module Breakdown
   155→
   156→### tc24r-codegen Modules
   157→
   158→| Module | Purpose |
   159→|--------|---------|
   160→| lib.rs | Top-level codegen entry, _start, globals, runtime helpers |
   161→| func.rs | Function prologue/epilogue, ISR variant |
   162→| stmt.rs | Statement dispatch (if, while, for, return, decl, assign) |
   163→| expr.rs | Expression dispatch (literals, variables, unary, call, cast) |
   164→| binop.rs | Binary operator code generation |
   165→| emit.rs | Assembly text emission helpers |
   166→| runtime.rs | __tc24r_div and __tc24r_mod implementations |
   167→
   168→### tc24r-parser Modules
   169→
   170→| Module | Purpose |
   171→|--------|---------|
   172→| lib.rs | Top-level parse entry, function and global parsing |
   173→| decl.rs | Type parsing, variable declarations |
   174→| stmt.rs | Statement parsing |
   175→| expr.rs | Expression parsing, precedence climbing |
   176→| arithmetic.rs | Arithmetic and comparison subexpressions |
   177→| bitwise.rs | Bitwise operator subexpressions |
   178→| control.rs | if, while, for, do...while parsing |
   179→
   180→### tc24r-lexer Modules
   181→
   182→| Module | Purpose |
   183→|--------|---------|
   184→| lib.rs | Main tokenizer loop |
   185→| operators.rs | Multi-character operator recognition |
   186→| readers.rs | String, number, identifier readers |
   187→

     3→/* tml.h -- Tiny Macro Lisp core types and macros
     4→ *
     5→ * All values are a single COR24 int (24-bit).
     6→ * Low 2 bits = tag, upper bits = payload.
     7→ *
     8→ *   [23..2] payload  [1:0] tag
     9→ *   00  Fixnum   -- 22-bit signed integer
    10→ *   01  Cons     -- heap cell index
    11→ *   10  Symbol   -- symbol table index
    12→ *   11  Extended -- heap cell index (closure, macro, primitive, string)
    13→ */
    14→
    15→/* Tags */
    16→#define TAG_FIXNUM   0
    17→#define TAG_CONS     1
    18→#define TAG_SYMBOL   2
    19→#define TAG_EXTENDED 3
    20→#define TAG_MASK     3
    21→
    22→/* Tag checks */
    23→#define IS_FIXNUM(v)   (((v) & TAG_MASK) == TAG_FIXNUM)
    24→#define IS_CONS(v)     (((v) & TAG_MASK) == TAG_CONS)
    25→#define IS_SYMBOL(v)   (((v) & TAG_MASK) == TAG_SYMBOL)
    26→#define IS_EXTENDED(v) (((v) & TAG_MASK) == TAG_EXTENDED)
    27→
    28→/* Fixnum encode/decode */
    29→#define MAKE_FIXNUM(n)  (((n) << 2) | TAG_FIXNUM)
    30→#define FIXNUM_VAL(v)   ((v) >> 2)
    31→
    32→/* Pointer (index) encode/decode */
    33→#define MAKE_CONS(idx)     (((idx) << 2) | TAG_CONS)
    34→#define MAKE_SYMBOL(idx)   (((idx) << 2) | TAG_SYMBOL)
    35→#define MAKE_EXTENDED(idx) (((idx) << 2) | TAG_EXTENDED)
    36→#define PTR_IDX(v)         ((v) >> 2)
    37→
    38→/* Special values: nil = symbol 0, t = symbol 1 */
    39→#define NIL_VAL   MAKE_SYMBOL(0)
    40→#define T_VAL     MAKE_SYMBOL(1)
    41→#define IS_NIL(v) ((v) == NIL_VAL)
    42→
    43→#define HEAP_SIZE 4096
    44→
    45→/* Extended object type tags */
    46→#define ETYPE_CLOSURE   MAKE_FIXNUM(1)
    47→#define ETYPE_MACRO     MAKE_FIXNUM(2)
    48→#define ETYPE_PRIMITIVE MAKE_FIXNUM(3)

1→# tc24r -- Tiny C Compiler for the COR24 FPGA Soft CPU
     2→
     3→tc24r is an open-source C compiler targeting the **COR24** (C-Oriented RISC, 24-bit) instruction set architecture. COR24 is an FPGA soft CPU designed by [MakerLisp](https://makerlisp.com) for efficient C code execution. The existing MakerLisp C compiler (cc24) is proprietary -- tc24r provides an open-source alternative.
     4→
     5→The approach is inspired by [chibicc](https://github.com/rui314/chibicc), a small educational C compiler. tc24r follows the same architecture pattern (recursive-descent parser, direct assembly emission) but is written from scratch in Rust.
     6→
     7→## Project Status
     8→
     9→tc24r is **functional** -- it compiles real C programs to COR24 assembly that runs on hardware and the cor24-rs emulator. 14 components, ~50 crates, 46 demos all passing. See [docs/status.md](docs/status.md) for detailed status and test counts.
    10→
    11→### What Works
    12→
    13→- Types: int (24-bit), unsigned int, char (8-bit), void, pointers, arrays
    14→- All standard C operators with correct precedence
    15→- Control flow: if/else, while, do...while, for, break, continue, switch/case
    16→- Ternary operator (? :), character literals, multi-declaration
    17→- Prefix/postfix increment and decrement (++i, i--)
    18→- Compound assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=, &=, |=, ^=, <<=, >>=
    19→- sizeof, typedef, enum, struct (dot and arrow access), union
    20→- Function prototypes (forward declarations, mutual recursion)
    21→- Functions with multiple parameters, recursion, ISR support
    22→- Globals, string constants, hex literals
    23→- Pointer arithmetic with element-size scaling
    24→- Preprocessor: #define, #include, #pragma once
    25→- Inline assembly: asm("...")
    26→- MMIO: LED, UART TX/RX, interrupt enable
    27→- Software division and modulo runtime
    28→
    29→### What Does Not Work Yet
    30→
    31→- Multi-file compilation
    32→- float/double (COR24 has no FPU -- out of scope)
    33→
    34→## COR24 Architecture at a Glance
    35→
    36→- **24-bit** registers and address space (16 MB)
    37→- **8 registers**: r0-r2 (general purpose), fp, sp, z (zero), iv, ir
    38→- **32 instructions**: arithmetic, logic, shift, compare, branch, load/store, stack ops
    39→- **1/2/4-byte** variable-length instruction encoding
    40→- Hardware multiply (24-cycle), no hardware divide
    41→- Little-endian, byte-addressable memory
    42→- UART and GPIO via memory-mapped I/O
    43→
    44→## Component Structure
    45→
    46→The compiler is organized into 14 components with ~50 crates, each component
    47→being its own Cargo workspace under `components/`:
    48→
    49→| Component | Purpose |
    50→|-----------|---------|
    51→| core | Shared types: AST, tokens, spans, errors, traits |
    52→| frontend | Preprocessing, lexing, parsing |
    53→| backend | Legacy codegen and validation |
    54→| codegen-emit | Assembly emission (core, data, load/store) |
    55→| codegen-expr | Expression codegen (call, literal, ops, pointer, variable) |
    56→| codegen-ops | Operator codegen (arithmetic, bitwise, compare, divmod, incdec, logical, unary, type-infer) |
    57→| codegen-state | Codegen state management |
    58→| codegen-stmt | Statement codegen (control flow, simple statements) |
    59→| codegen-structure | Function structure (ISR, locals, prologue) |
    60→| config | Configuration and target definition |
    61→| dispatch | Codegen dispatch layer |
    62→| macros | Proc macros (asm-dsl, emit-macros, handler-macros) |
    63→| testing | Test harnesses (as24, compile, cor24, golden) |
    64→| cli | CLI binary entry point |
    65→
    66→See [docs/architecture.md](docs/architecture.md) for data flow and design constraints.
    67→
    68→## Building
    69→
    70→Build all components:
    71→
    72→```bash
    73→scripts/build-all.sh
    74→```
    75→
    76→This runs each component's `scripts/build.sh` in dependency order. The release binary is produced at:
    77→
    78→```
    79→components/cli/target/release/tc24r
    80→```
    81→
    82→To build a single component:
    83→
    84→```bash
    85→cargo build --manifest-path components/<name>/Cargo.toml
    86→```
    87→
    88→## Usage
    89→
    90→```bash
    91→tc24r <input.c> [-o output.s] [-I dir]
    92→```
    93→
    94→- `<input.c>` -- C source file to compile
    95→- `-o output.s` -- output assembly file (default: stdout)
    96→- `-I dir` -- add include search path (repeatable)
    97→
    98→### Full Workflow
    99→
   100→```bash
   101→# Compile C to COR24 assembly
   102→tc24r program.c -o program.s
   103→
   104→# Assemble (requires as24 from COR24-TB archive)
   105→as24 < program.s | longlgo > program.lgo
   106→
   107→# Run on emulator (cor24-rs)
   108→cd ~/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs
   109→cargo run -- run path/to/program.s
   110→```
   111→
   112→## Testing
   113→
   114→| Test Suite | Pass | Total | Notes |
   115→|-----------|------|-------|-------|
   116→| tc24r demos | 46 | 46 | End-to-end compiler + emulator |
   117→| chibicc-subset | 5 | 5 | Curated subsets of chibicc tests |
   118→| chibicc full | 6 | 41 | const, decl, enum, generic, pragma-once, stdhdr |
   119→| beej-c-guide | 4 | 11 | hello_world, functions, pointers, typedef |
   120→| bgc examples | 41 | 117 | With stdio/stdlib/string/stdbool stubs |
   121→
   122→See [Testing Status](docs/testing-status.md) for detailed tables and blocker analysis.
   123→

   125→demos/run-demo.sh              # run a single demo
   126→scripts/run-subset-tests.sh    # run chibicc-subset tests
   127→scripts/run-chibicc-tests.sh   # run full chibicc tests
   128→```

     5→//!   0x100000 - 0xFDFFFF  Unmapped (addressable, returns 0)
     6→//!   0xFEE000 - 0xFEFFFF  Embedded Block RAM (8 KB window, 3 KB populated)
     7→//!   0xFF0000 - 0xFFFFFF  I/O space

    16→
    17→/// EBR (Embedded Block RAM) base address
    18→pub const EBR_BASE: u32 = 0xFEE000;
    19→

1→# Detailed Design: Tiny Macro Lisp (tml24c)
     2→
     3→## Data Representation
     4→
     5→### Tagged Values
     6→
     7→All Lisp values are represented as a single 24-bit word (`typedef int Value;` on COR24, `typedef int32_t Value;` on host).
     8→
     9→```
    10→Bit layout (24 bits):
    11→[23 ..................... 2][1][0]
    12→         payload            tag
    13→```
    14→
    15→| Tag | Type | Payload |
    16→|-----|------|---------|
    17→| 00 | Fixnum | 22-bit signed integer (arithmetic shift right 2 to decode) |
    18→| 01 | Cons | Heap index (cell number, not byte address) |
    19→| 10 | Symbol | Heap index |
    20→| 11 | Extended | Heap index → object with runtime type header |
    21→
    22→**Fixnum encoding:**
    23→```c
    24→#define MAKE_FIXNUM(n)   (((n) << 2) | TAG_FIXNUM)
    25→#define FIXNUM_VAL(v)    ((v) >> 2)           // arithmetic shift preserves sign
    26→#define IS_FIXNUM(v)     (((v) & 3) == TAG_FIXNUM)
    27→```
    28→
    29→**Pointer encoding:**
    30→```c
    31→#define MAKE_CONS(idx)   (((idx) << 2) | TAG_CONS)
    32→#define CONS_IDX(v)      ((v) >> 2)
    33→#define IS_CONS(v)       (((v) & 3) == TAG_CONS)
    34→```
    35→
    36→### Special Values
    37→
    38→```c
    39→#define NIL_VAL          MAKE_SYMBOL(0)   // symbol index 0 = nil
    40→#define T_VAL            MAKE_SYMBOL(1)   // symbol index 1 = t
    41→#define IS_NIL(v)        ((v) == NIL_VAL)
    42→#define IS_TRUE(v)       (!IS_NIL(v))     // everything non-nil is true
    43→```
    44→
    45→### Fixnum Range
    46→
    47→22-bit signed: -2,097,152 to 2,097,151. Sufficient for demos, loop counters, list indices. Overflow wraps silently (no bignum fallback).
    48→
    49→## Heap Layout
    50→
    51→### Cell Array
    52→
    53→The heap is a fixed array of cells. Each cell is 6 bytes (two 24-bit words).
    54→
    55→```c
    56→typedef struct {
    57→    Value car;
    58→    Value cdr;
    59→} Cell;
    60→
    61→#define HEAP_SIZE 8192   // tunable; 8K cells = 48 KB
    62→Cell heap[HEAP_SIZE];
    63→```
    64→
    65→All heap-allocated objects occupy one or more cells:
    66→
    67→| Object | Cells | Layout |
    68→|--------|-------|--------|
    69→| Cons pair | 1 | car=first, cdr=rest |
    70→| Symbol | 1 | car=name_string, cdr=next_symbol |
    71→| Closure | 2 | cell0: car=params, cdr=body; cell1: car=env, cdr=type_tag |
    72→| Macro | 2 | Same as closure, different type_tag |
    73→| Primitive | 1 | car=function_index, cdr=type_tag |
    74→| String | 1+ | car=length, cdr=pointer to char storage |
    75→
    76→### Free List
    77→
    78→After GC initialization, all unused cells form a singly-linked free list via the `cdr` field:
    79→
    80→```
    81→free_list → cell[N] → cell[M] → cell[K] → NIL
    82→```
    83→
    84→Allocation pops from the free list. Sweep rebuilds it.
    85→
    86→### Extended Object Type Tags
    87→
    88→Objects tagged `11` (extended) carry a type discriminator in their second cell's `cdr`:
    89→
    90→```c
    91→#define ETYPE_CLOSURE    MAKE_FIXNUM(1)
    92→#define ETYPE_MACRO      MAKE_FIXNUM(2)
    93→#define ETYPE_PRIMITIVE  MAKE_FIXNUM(3)
    94→#define ETYPE_STRING     MAKE_FIXNUM(4)
    95→```
    96→
    97→## Environment Structure
    98→
    99→### Frame Representation
   100→
   101→An environment is a tagged cons pointer to a list of frames. Each frame is a cons pair:
   102→- `car` = association list of (symbol . value) pairs
   103→- `cdr` = parent environment (or NIL for global)
   104→
   105→```
   106→env → (frame . parent_env)
   107→       │
   108→       ▼
   109→      ((x . 10) (y . 20) . nil)
   110→```
   111→
   112→### Operations
   113→
   114→```c
   115→Value env_lookup(Value sym, Value env);     // walk chain, error if unbound
   116→Value env_define(Value sym, Value val, Value env);  // add to current frame
   117→Value env_set(Value sym, Value val, Value env);     // update existing binding
   118→Value env_extend(Value params, Value args, Value env); // new frame with bindings
   119→```
   120→
   121→### Global Environment
   122→
   123→The global environment is a single frame containing all built-in primitives and user `define`d values. Initialized at startup.
   124→
   125→## Closure Representation
   126→
   127→A closure captures: parameter list, body, and the environment at definition time.
   128→
   129→```
   130→Extended object (2 cells):
   131→  Cell 0: car = param_list,  cdr = body
   132→  Cell 1: car = captured_env, cdr = ETYPE_CLOSURE
   133→```
   134→
   135→**Application:**
   136→```
   137→apply(closure, args):
   138→  new_env = env_extend(closure.params, args, closure.env)
   139→  return eval_body(closure.body, new_env)
   140→```
   141→
   142→## Macro Representation
   143→
   144→Identical to closure but tagged `ETYPE_MACRO`. During eval, if the operator resolves to a macro:
   145→
   146→1. **Do not** evaluate the arguments
   147→2. Apply the macro function to the raw argument forms
   148→3. Evaluate the resulting expansion in the original environment
   149→
   150→```
   151→eval((op . args), env):
   152→  op_val = eval(op, env)
   153→  if is_macro(op_val):
   154→    expanded = apply_macro(op_val, args)  // args NOT evaluated
   155→    return eval(expanded, env)
   156→```
   157→
   158→## Macro Expansion Strategy
   159→
   160→### defmacro
   161→
   162→```lisp
   163→(defmacro when (test . body)
   164→  `(if ,test (do ,@body) nil))
   165→```
   166→
   167→Binds a macro object in the global environment. The macro's body is a closure that receives unevaluated argument forms and returns a new form.
   168→
   169→### Quasiquote
   170→
   171→Implemented in the reader as syntactic sugar:
   172→
   173→| Syntax | Reads as |
   174→|--------|----------|
   175→| `` `x `` | `(quasiquote x)` |
   176→| `,x` | `(unquote x)` |
   177→| `,@x` | `(unquote-splicing x)` |
   178→
   179→The evaluator handles `quasiquote` as a special form that recursively processes the template:
   180→- Literal forms → quoted
   181→- `(unquote x)` → evaluate x
   182→- `(unquote-splicing x)` → evaluate x, splice into list
   183→
   184→### gensym
   185→
   186→```c
   187→static int gensym_counter = 0;
   188→Value lisp_gensym(void) {
   189→    // Create uninterned symbol: G__0, G__1, etc.
   190→}
   191→```
   192→
   193→Generates unique symbols to avoid variable capture in macros.
   194→
   195→## Garbage Collector
   196→
   197→### Algorithm: Mark-Sweep
   198→
   199→**When triggered:** Allocation fails (free list empty).
   200→
   201→**Mark phase:**
   202→```
   203→mark(val):
   204→  if val is fixnum: return        // immediate, not on heap
   205→  idx = heap_index(val)
   206→  if mark_bit[idx]: return        // already marked
   207→  mark_bit[idx] = 1
   208→  if is_cons(val) or is_symbol(val):
   209→    mark(heap[idx].car)
   210→    mark(heap[idx].cdr)
   211→  if is_extended(val):
   212→    mark(heap[idx].car)
   213→    mark(heap[idx].cdr)
   214→    mark(heap[idx+1].car)         // second cell
   215→    mark(heap[idx+1].cdr)
   216→```
   217→
   218→**Sweep phase:**
   219→```
   220→sweep():
   221→  free_list = NIL
   222→  for i = HEAP_SIZE-1 downto 0:
   223→    if mark_bit[i]:
   224→      mark_bit[i] = 0            // clear for next cycle
   225→    else:
   226→      heap[i].cdr = free_list    // add to free list
   227→      free_list = i
   228→```
   229→
   230→### Mark Bit Storage
   231→
   232→Separate bitmap array: `char mark_bits[(HEAP_SIZE + 7) / 8]`
   233→
   234→One bit per cell. Avoids disturbing object layout. Costs HEAP_SIZE/8 bytes (1 KB for 8K cells).
   235→
   236→### Root Enumeration
   237→
   238→Roots that must be scanned:
   239→1. `global_env` — the global environment
   240→2. `temp_roots[]` — a small stack of values being constructed (reader, eval temps)
   241→3. `symbol_table` — the interned symbol list
   242→4. Evaluation stack — values on the C call stack during recursive eval
   243→
   244→**C stack scanning strategy:** Use a "temp root" stack. Before any allocation that might trigger GC, push live values onto `temp_roots`. Pop after use.
   245→
   246→```c
   247→#define PUSH_ROOT(v)  (temp_roots[temp_root_count++] = (v))
   248→#define POP_ROOT()    (temp_root_count--)
   249→```
   250→
   251→This avoids conservative stack scanning.
   252→
   253→## Special Forms
   254→
   255→| Form | Syntax | Semantics |
   256→|------|--------|-----------|
   257→| `quote` | `(quote x)` or `'x` | Return x unevaluated |
   258→| `if` | `(if test then else)` | Conditional; else optional (defaults to nil) |
   259→| `define` | `(define sym expr)` | Bind in current env |
   260→| `set!` | `(set! sym expr)` | Mutate existing binding |
   261→| `lambda` | `(lambda (params) body...)` | Create closure |
   262→| `defmacro` | `(defmacro name (params) body...)` | Create macro |
   263→| `do` | `(do expr1 expr2 ...)` | Evaluate sequentially, return last |
   264→| `let` | `(let ((x 1) (y 2)) body...)` | Local bindings (parallel) |
   265→| `let*` | `(let* ((x 1) (y x)) body...)` | Local bindings (sequential) |
   266→| `quasiquote` | `` `(a ,b ,@c) `` | Template with interpolation |
   267→
   268→`let` and `let*` may be implemented as macros expanding to lambda applications, or as direct special forms. Direct special forms are simpler for the evaluator.
   269→
   270→## Built-in Primitives
   271→
   272→### List Operations
   273→- `cons`, `car`, `cdr`
   274→- `list` (variadic: `(list 1 2 3)`)
   275→
   276→### Predicates
   277→- `eq?` — pointer equality
   278→- `null?` — is nil
   279→- `atom?` — not a cons pair
   280→- `symbol?`, `number?`, `string?`, `pair?`
   281→
   282→### Arithmetic
   283→- `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `mod`
   284→- `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `=` (numeric equality)
   285→
   286→### I/O
   287→- `print` — print value without newline
   288→- `println` — print value with newline
   289→- `read` — read one S-expression from input
   290→- `display` — print strings without quotes
   291→
   292→### Meta
   293→- `gensym` — generate unique symbol
   294→- `macroexpand-1` — expand one macro step
   295→- `eval` — evaluate a form
   296→- `apply` — apply function to argument list
   297→
   298→### String (Phase 2)
   299→- `string-length`, `string-ref`, `string-append`
   300→- `string=?`, `string<?`
   301→- `number->string`, `symbol->string`
   302→
   303→## Error Handling
   304→
   305→Minimal error handling for bootstrap:
   306→- Print error message to UART/stdout
   307→- Halt (COR24) or longjmp to REPL top-level (host)
   308→
   309→No condition/restart system. No stack traces initially.
   310→
   311→```c
   312→void lisp_error(const char *msg, Value val);
   313→// Prints: "Error: <msg> <val>" then halts or returns to REPL
   314→```
   315→
   316→On host, use `setjmp`/`longjmp` for error recovery to REPL. On COR24, initially just halt; later add REPL recovery.
   317→
   318→## Reader Design
   319→
   320→### Tokenizer States
   321→1. Skip whitespace and comments (`;` to end of line)
   322→2. `(` → open paren
   323→3. `)` → close paren
   324→4. `'` → read next, wrap in `(quote ...)`
   325→5. `` ` `` → read next, wrap in `(quasiquote ...)`
   326→6. `,` → peek for `@`; wrap in `(unquote ...)` or `(unquote-splicing ...)`
   327→7. `"` → read string literal with escape sequences
   328→8. `-` or digit → read integer
   329→9. Other → read symbol (alphanumeric + `!?*+-/<>=_`)
   330→
   331→### List Reader
   332→```
   333→read_list():
   334→  tok = peek()
   335→  if tok == ')': consume, return NIL
   336→  first = read_expr()
   337→  rest = read_list()
   338→  return cons(first, rest)
   339→```
   340→
   341→No dotted-pair syntax initially. Add later if needed.
   342→

   344→

1→# Implementation Plan: Tiny Macro Lisp (tml24c)
     2→
     3→## Overview
     4→
     5→Six phases, each building on the last. Host-first development — all phases run on host (gcc/clang) before COR24 porting.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## Phase 0: Project Scaffold
    10→
    11→**Goal:** Buildable C project with Makefile, header, and platform abstraction.
    12→
    13→**Steps:**
    14→1. Create `src/tml.h` — Value typedef, tag constants, forward declarations
    15→2. Create `src/platform_host.c` — putchar/getchar/halt via stdio
    16→3. Create `src/main.c` — skeleton main with init and test stub
    17→4. Create/update `Makefile` — compile all .c in src/, link, `-Wall -Wextra -Werror -std=c11`
    18→5. Verify: `make clean && make` succeeds with zero warnings
    19→
    20→**Deliverable:** Empty program that compiles and runs, prints "tml24c ok".
    21→
    22→**AgentRail step:** `003-scaffold` / task type: `c-project-init`
    23→
    24→---
    25→
    26→## Phase 1: Core Evaluator (No GC, No Macros)
    27→
    28→### Phase 1a: Heap + Symbols + Cons
    29→
    30→**Goal:** Allocate cons cells and intern symbols. Bump allocator (no free/GC yet).
    31→
    32→**Steps:**
    33→1. Implement `heap.c` — cell array, `alloc_cell()` bump allocator, `alloc_cells(n)` for multi-cell objects
    34→2. Implement `symbol.c` — `intern(name)` returns symbol Value; linear search of symbol list
    35→3. Add fixnum/cons/symbol constructors and accessors to `tml.h`
    36→4. Test: construct `(a b c)` in C, verify tag checks
    37→
    38→### Phase 1b: Reader
    39→
    40→**Goal:** Parse text into Value trees.
    41→
    42→**Steps:**
    43→1. Implement `read.c` — `read_expr()` dispatching on first character
    44→2. Handle: whitespace, `;` comments, `(` lists `)`, `'` quote shorthand
    45→3. Handle: integer literals, symbol names
    46→4. Test from C: `read_expr("(+ 1 2)")` produces correct cons structure
    47→
    48→**AgentRail step:** `005-reader` / task type: `lisp-define-form`
    49→
    50→### Phase 1c: Printer
    51→
    52→**Goal:** Print Values as text.

    54→**Steps:**
    55→1. Implement `print.c` — `print_val(v)` dispatching on tag
    56→2. Handle: fixnums, symbols, proper lists, nil
    57→3. Test: print what reader produced, verify round-trip
    58→
    59→### Phase 1d: Environment + Evaluator
    60→
    61→**Goal:** Evaluate expressions with global bindings.
    62→
    63→**Steps:**
    64→1. Implement `env.c` — `env_lookup`, `env_define`, `env_set`, `env_extend`
    65→2. Implement `eval.c` — `eval(expr, env)` with:
    66→   - Self-evaluating fixnums
    67→   - Symbol lookup
    68→   - Special forms: `quote`, `if`, `define`, `lambda`, `do`
    69→   - Function application (closure + primitive dispatch)
    70→3. Implement `builtin.c` — register primitives: cons, car, cdr, eq?, atom?, null?, +, -, *, <, print
    71→4. Implement closure creation and application
    72→5. Wire up REPL in `main.c`: read → eval → print loop
    73→
    74→**Tests (entered at REPL or eval'd from C strings):**
    75→```lisp

    77→(+ 1 2)                    ;=> 3

    80→(if (< 1 2) 'yes 'no)     ;=> yes

    89→### Phase 1e: Lexical Closures
    90→

    98→```

   101→
   102→---
   103→
   104→## Phase 2: Garbage Collector
   105→
   106→**Goal:** Mark-sweep GC so programs can allocate indefinitely.
   107→
   108→**Steps:**
   109→1. Add mark bitmap to `heap.c`
   110→2. Implement `gc.c` — `gc_mark(val)` recursive marker, `gc_sweep()` free-list builder
   111→3. Add temp-root stack (`PUSH_ROOT`/`POP_ROOT`) to protect live values during allocation
   112→4. Instrument `alloc_cell()`: if free list empty, trigger GC; if still empty, error

   126→

   128→
   129→## Phase 3: Macros
   130→
   131→**Goal:** `defmacro`, quasiquote, gensym. Bootstrap library in Lisp.
   132→
   133→### Phase 3a: defmacro + Quasiquote

   137→2. Add `defmacro` special form to eval

   140→5. Implement `gensym`
   141→
   142→**Test:**
   143→```lisp
   144→(defmacro when (test . body)
   145→  `(if ,test (do ,@body) nil))
   146→(when (< 1 2) (print 'hello) 'yes)  ;=> yes (prints hello)

   148→
   149→### Phase 3b: Bootstrap Library
   150→
   151→**Steps:**
   152→1. Create `lib/stdlib.lisp` with:

   176→## Phase 4: Strings

   178→**Goal:** String type for practical programs and better error messages.

1→//! COR24 instruction opcodes and formats.
     2→
     3→/// COR24 instruction opcodes (5-bit, from decoded ROM)
     4→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
     5→#[cfg_attr(feature = "serde", derive(serde::Serialize, serde::Deserialize))]
     6→#[repr(u8)]
     7→pub enum Opcode {
     8→    AddReg = 0x00, // add ra,rb
     9→    AddImm = 0x01, // add ra,dd
    10→    And = 0x02,    // and ra,rb
    11→    Bra = 0x03,    // bra dd
    12→    Brf = 0x04,    // brf dd
    13→    Brt = 0x05,    // brt dd
    14→    Ceq = 0x06,    // ceq ra,rb
    15→    Cls = 0x07,    // cls ra,rb (compare less signed)
    16→    Clu = 0x08,    // clu ra,rb (compare less unsigned)
    17→    Jal = 0x09,    // jal ra,(rb)
    18→    Jmp = 0x0A,    // jmp (ra)
    19→    La = 0x0B,     // la ra,dddddd
    20→    Lb = 0x0C,     // lb ra,dd(rb) (signed)
    21→    Lbu = 0x0D,    // lbu ra,dd(rb) (unsigned)
    22→    Lc = 0x0E,     // lc ra,dd (signed)
    23→    Lcu = 0x0F,    // lcu ra,dd (unsigned)
    24→    Lw = 0x10,     // lw ra,dd(rb)
    25→    Mov = 0x11,    // mov ra,rb or mov ra,c
    26→    Mul = 0x12,    // mul ra,rb
    27→    Or = 0x13,     // or ra,rb
    28→    Pop = 0x14,    // pop ra
    29→    Push = 0x15,   // push ra
    30→    Sb = 0x16,     // sb ra,dd(rb)
    31→    Shl = 0x17,    // shl ra,rb
    32→    Sra = 0x18,    // sra ra,rb
    33→    Srl = 0x19,    // srl ra,rb
    34→    Sub = 0x1A,    // sub ra,rb
    35→    SubSp = 0x1B,  // sub sp,dddddd
    36→    Sw = 0x1C,     // sw ra,dd(rb)
    37→    Sxt = 0x1D,    // sxt ra,rb
    38→    Xor = 0x1E,    // xor ra,rb
    39→    Zxt = 0x1F,    // zxt ra,rb
    40→    Invalid = 0xFF,
    41→}
    42→
    43→impl From<u8> for Opcode {
    44→    fn from(value: u8) -> Self {
    45→        match value {
    46→            0x00 => Opcode::AddReg,
    47→            0x01 => Opcode::AddImm,
    48→            0x02 => Opcode::And,
    49→            0x03 => Opcode::Bra,
    50→            0x04 => Opcode::Brf,
    51→            0x05 => Opcode::Brt,
    52→            0x06 => Opcode::Ceq,
    53→            0x07 => Opcode::Cls,
    54→            0x08 => Opcode::Clu,
    55→            0x09 => Opcode::Jal,
    56→            0x0A => Opcode::Jmp,
    57→            0x0B => Opcode::La,
    58→            0x0C => Opcode::Lb,
    59→            0x0D => Opcode::Lbu,
    60→            0x0E => Opcode::Lc,
    61→            0x0F => Opcode::Lcu,
    62→            0x10 => Opcode::Lw,
    63→            0x11 => Opcode::Mov,
    64→            0x12 => Opcode::Mul,
    65→            0x13 => Opcode::Or,
    66→            0x14 => Opcode::Pop,
    67→            0x15 => Opcode::Push,
    68→            0x16 => Opcode::Sb,
    69→            0x17 => Opcode::Shl,
    70→            0x18 => Opcode::Sra,
    71→            0x19 => Opcode::Srl,
    72→            0x1A => Opcode::Sub,
    73→            0x1B => Opcode::SubSp,
    74→            0x1C => Opcode::Sw,
    75→            0x1D => Opcode::Sxt,
    76→            0x1E => Opcode::Xor,
    77→            0x1F => Opcode::Zxt,
    78→            _ => Opcode::Invalid,
    79→        }
    80→    }
    81→}
    82→
    83→/// Decoded instruction with opcode and register operands
    84→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
    85→pub struct DecodedInstruction {
    86→    pub opcode: Opcode,
    87→    pub ra: u8,
    88→    pub rb: u8,
    89→}
    90→
    91→impl DecodedInstruction {
    92→    pub fn new(opcode: Opcode, ra: u8, rb: u8) -> Self {
    93→        Self {
    94→            opcode,
    95→            ra: ra & 0x07,
    96→            rb: rb & 0x07,
    97→        }
    98→    }
    99→
   100→    /// Decode from 12-bit ROM output
   101→    pub fn from_decoded(decoded: u16) -> Self {
   102→        let opcode = Opcode::from(((decoded >> 6) & 0x1F) as u8);
   103→        let ra = ((decoded >> 3) & 0x07) as u8;
   104→        let rb = (decoded & 0x07) as u8;
   105→        Self { opcode, ra, rb }
   106→    }
   107→}
   108→
   109→/// Instruction format type
   110→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
   111→pub enum InstructionFormat {
   112→    /// Single byte: opcode + registers encoded
   113→    SingleByte,
   114→    /// Two bytes: first byte + 8-bit immediate/offset
   115→    TwoBytes,
   116→    /// Four bytes: first byte + 24-bit address
   117→    FourBytes,
   118→}
   119→
   120→impl InstructionFormat {
   121→    /// Size in bytes
   122→    pub fn size(&self) -> usize {
   123→        match self {
   124→            InstructionFormat::SingleByte => 1,
   125→            InstructionFormat::TwoBytes => 2,
   126→            InstructionFormat::FourBytes => 4,
   127→        }
   128→    }
   129→}
   130→
   131→impl Opcode {
   132→    /// Get the instruction format for this opcode
   133→    pub fn format(&self) -> InstructionFormat {
   134→        match self {
   135→            Opcode::AddReg | Opcode::And | Opcode::Ceq | Opcode::Cls | Opcode::Clu
   136→            | Opcode::Jal | Opcode::Jmp | Opcode::Mov | Opcode::Mul | Opcode::Or
   137→            | Opcode::Pop | Opcode::Push | Opcode::Shl | Opcode::Sra | Opcode::Srl
   138→            | Opcode::Sub | Opcode::Sxt | Opcode::Xor | Opcode::Zxt => {
   139→                InstructionFormat::SingleByte
   140→            }
   141→            Opcode::AddImm | Opcode::Bra | Opcode::Brf | Opcode::Brt | Opcode::Lb
   142→            | Opcode::Lbu | Opcode::Lc | Opcode::Lcu | Opcode::Lw | Opcode::Sb
   143→            | Opcode::Sw => InstructionFormat::TwoBytes,
   144→            Opcode::La | Opcode::SubSp => InstructionFormat::FourBytes,
   145→            Opcode::Invalid => InstructionFormat::SingleByte,
   146→        }
   147→    }
   148→
   149→    /// Get mnemonic string for this opcode
   150→    pub fn mnemonic(&self) -> &'static str {
   151→        match self {
   152→            Opcode::AddReg | Opcode::AddImm => "add",
   153→            Opcode::And => "and",
   154→            Opcode::Bra => "bra",
   155→            Opcode::Brf => "brf",
   156→            Opcode::Brt => "brt",
   157→            Opcode::Ceq => "ceq",
   158→            Opcode::Cls => "cls",
   159→            Opcode::Clu => "clu",
   160→            Opcode::Jal => "jal",
   161→            Opcode::Jmp => "jmp",
   162→            Opcode::La => "la",
   163→            Opcode::Lb => "lb",
   164→            Opcode::Lbu => "lbu",
   165→            Opcode::Lc => "lc",
   166→            Opcode::Lcu => "lcu",
   167→            Opcode::Lw => "lw",
   168→            Opcode::Mov => "mov",
   169→            Opcode::Mul => "mul",
   170→            Opcode::Or => "or",
   171→            Opcode::Pop => "pop",
   172→            Opcode::Push => "push",
   173→            Opcode::Sb => "sb",
   174→            Opcode::Shl => "shl",
   175→            Opcode::Sra => "sra",
   176→            Opcode::Srl => "srl",
   177→            Opcode::Sub | Opcode::SubSp => "sub",
   178→            Opcode::Sw => "sw",
   179→            Opcode::Sxt => "sxt",
   180→            Opcode::Xor => "xor",
   181→            Opcode::Zxt => "zxt",
   182→            Opcode::Invalid => "???",
   183→        }
   184→    }
   185→}

    92→/// Unary operator.
    93→#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq)]
    94→pub enum UnaryOp {
    95→    Neg,
    96→    BitNot,
    97→    LogNot,
    98→}
